questions.text: A car is traveling with uniform acceleration along a straight road. The road has marker posts every 100 m. When the car passes one post, it has a speed of 10 m/s and when it passes the next one, its speed is 20 m/s. What is the car's acceleration?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Calculations show acceleration is 1.5m/s² . This makes option A incorrect.

A car is travelling with uniform acceleration along a straight road. The road has marker posts every 100 m, d = 100 mInitial speed of the car, u = 10 m/sFinal speed of the car, v = 20 m/sLet a is the acceleration of the car. It is equal to the rate of change of velocity per unit time.
Calculations show acceleration is 1.5m/s² . This makes option C incorrect.
Calculations show acceleration is 1.5m/s² . This makes option D incorrect.

questions.explanation: A car is travelling with uniform acceleration along a straight road. The road has marker posts every 100 m, d = 100 mInitial speed of the car, u = 10 m/sFinal speed of the car, v = 20 m/sLet a is the acceleration of the car. It is equal to the rate of change of velocity per unit time.

questions.text: A wire can sustain the weight of 20kg before breaking. If the wire is cut into two equal parts, each part can sustain a weight of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: ❌ Wrong because this assumes that shorter wire = weaker wire.But length doesn’t reduce strength unless area changes.
The breaking stress in each part of the wire will be the same as the original because of the same material. Since the area remains the same each part can sustain a weight of 20 kg.
❌ Wrong because no extra strength is added by cutting the wire.The physical property (strength) doesn't double.
❌ Incorrect and exaggerated. Cutting the wire doesn’t quadruple its strength.

questions.explanation: The breaking stress in each part of the wire will be the same as the original because of the same material. Since the area remains the same each part can sustain a weight of 20 kg.

questions.text: If we drop an object, it’s initial velocity is zero. How far will it fall in time ‘t’?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: ❌ Incorrect. This would be the correct answer if the object had initial velocity of 9.8 m/s, but here initial velocity is 0.

Free fall is a motion of a body in which the only force acting of the body is the Gravitational Force. In this motion body moves with constant acceleration i.e acceleration due to gravity.When an object is thrown downward from a non zero height say h the motion is referred to as free fall with initial velocity of object beign zero.Hence v = 0By using the kinematics straight line motion formula :s= u +½ at2s= 0 + ½ x 9.8x t2s= 4.9 t2
❌ Too small. This assumes gravity is 0.98 m/s² instead of 9.8.
❌ Too large. It’s ten times the correct value, and not based on the formula.

questions.explanation: Free fall is a motion of a body in which the only force acting of the body is the Gravitational Force. In this motion body moves with constant acceleration i.e acceleration due to gravity.When an object is thrown downward from a non zero height say h the motion is referred to as free fall with initial velocity of object beign zero.Hence v = 0By using the kinematics straight line motion formula : s= u +½ at2 s= 0 + ½ x 9.8x t2 s= 4.9 t2

questions.text: An object is falling down with a speed of 20 m/s. After 3 seconds, its velocity will be _ m/s (g = 10 m/s2).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is too low and doesn’t follow from the equation v=u+gtv = u + gtv=u+gt. It may result from misapplying the formula.
Correct according to the solution.
Incorrect. This assumes either a wrong initial velocity or uses incorrect arithmetic. The object does not reach this speed under the given conditions.
Incorrect. This would be true only if the object started at a much higher initial velocity or fell for a longer time. It does not match the actual calculation.

questions.explanation: Given:Velocity = u = 20m/sTime = t = 3secSolution:v=u+atv=20+10(3)v=20+30=50 m/s.Result:Hence B.

questions.text: An object is falling down with a speed of 20 m/s. After 3 secondsits velocity will be _ m/s (g = 10 m/s2).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. It suggests the object has decelerated, which contradicts the scenario where gravity is accelerating the object.
This is correct. The object's velocity increases by 30 m/s due to gravity over 3 seconds.
This option is incorrect. It overestimates the increase in velocity by adding an extra 5 m/s.
This option is incorrect. It vastly overestimates the increase in velocity, confusing the scenario with one involving much higher acceleration or initial velocity.

questions.explanation: To find the final velocity of the object after 3 seconds of free fall, we use the kinematic equation: v = u + at. The initial velocity (u) is 20 m/s, the acceleration due to gravity (a) is 10 m/s2, and the time (t) is 3 seconds. Substituting these values into the equation, we get v = 20 + 10(3) = 20 + 30 = 50 m/s. Thus, the correct answer is 50 m/s. Option A (05) incorrectly assumes deceleration, Option C (55) miscalculates the velocity increase, and Option D (95) is an extreme overestimate.

questions.text: if a car starts from rest and reaches 20 m/s velocity in 10 s then acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This would be the acceleration if the final velocity was reached in 1 second (v = 20 m/s, t = 1 s).
This would be the acceleration if the velocity increased by 10 m/s in 1 second, which is not the case here.
This option assumes a different time frame or velocity change that does not match the given problem statement.
Correct! Using the formula v = u + at, where v = 20 m/s, u = 0 (since the car starts from rest), and t = 10 s, we find a = 2 m/s².

questions.explanation: The formula for uniformly accelerated motion is v = u + at. Here, the car starts from rest, so the initial velocity u = 0. The final velocity v = 20 m/s, and the time t = 10 s. Substituting these values into the formula gives 20 m/s = 0 + a(10 s), which simplifies to a = 20 m/s / 10 s = 2 m/s². Therefore, the correct answer is 2 m/s².Option A suggests an acceleration of 20 m/s², which would be correct if the velocity was reached in 1 second, not 10 seconds. Option B's 10 m/s² would suggest a much faster increase in velocity than given. Option C's 5 m/s² would imply a miscalculation of either time or velocity change.

questions.text: A plane is moving with speed v at a height 'h' in the horizontal direction. The time taken to reach the ground is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: If we solve the second equation of motion for time:h = ut + 1/2gt2Since it is moving with v velocity therefore the initial velocity u must have been zeroh = 1/2gt2t2 = 2h/gt = √2h/g

questions.text: In the absence of air resistance, an object thrown into the air at an angle of 20 degrees to the ground. It will experience:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the object will experience a constant acceleration in the vertical direction. In the absence of air resistance, the only force acting on the object vertically is gravity. Gravity causes a constant acceleration of approximately 9.8 m/s² downward, regardless of the object's initial velocity or angle of projection. Therefore, this option is correct. The vertical acceleration is indeed constant and equal to the acceleration due to gravity.

This option proposes that the vertical acceleration of the object changes over time. As mentioned above, in the absence of air resistance, the only force acting vertically is gravity, which provides a constant acceleration downward. Therefore, the vertical acceleration remains constant.

This option suggests that the horizontal acceleration of the object changes over time. However, in the absence of any horizontal forces (like air resistance or propulsion), the horizontal acceleration of the object remains zero. The object will continue moving horizontally with a constant velocity (which is determined by its initial velocity) while undergoing vertical acceleration due to gravity.

This option implies that the object experiences a non-zero horizontal acceleration. In the absence of any horizontal forces, the horizontal acceleration of the object is zero. The object's horizontal motion remains unchanged, with constant velocity, unless acted upon by an external horizontal force.

questions.explanation: The only acceleration a free-thrown object experiences is the gravitational acceleration, which at all times is constant (in this case) and is vertically towards the earth's surface.It will experience weightlessness, for a while, then a jolt, when it hits the ground (moves under the influence of gravitational acceleration).The angle is irrelevant: the experience will be the same regardless of the angle. The only difference that angle would make is how long the experience would last. Without the launch speed, the time that the experience lasts also remains unknown.

questions.text: Two bullets are fired simultaneously, horizontally and with a different speed from the same place. Which bullet will hit the ground first?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the horizontal speed does not influence the time it takes for an object to fall to the ground. Both bullets will experience the same gravitational force, causing them to hit the ground at the same time.

This option is incorrect because gravitational acceleration is the same for all objects regardless of their mass, in the absence of air resistance. Thus, mass does not affect the time they take to hit the ground.

This option is incorrect because, similar to the faster bullet, the slower bullet is also subject only to gravitational acceleration, not horizontal velocity, determining the time it takes to hit the ground.

Given, u1​>u2 ​In both cases, the downward acceleration and the initial vertical velocities are the same, they will take the same time to hit the ground.As we know, h=uy​t+0.5gt2 (Applying in vertical direction) h=0+0.5gt2t=√2h/g​​. The time of flight does not depend on the horizontal velocity.Hence both will reach simultaneously.

questions.explanation: Given, u1>u2, In both cases, the downward acceleration and the initial vertical velocities are the same, they will take the same time to hit the ground.As we know, h=uyt+0.5gt2 (Applying in the vertical direction) h=0+0.5gt2t=√2h/g​​. The time of flight does not depend on the horizontal velocity. Hence both will reach simultaneously.

questions.text: A body is thrown horizontally from the top of a tower of height 5m. It touches the ground at a distance of 10 m from the foot of the tower. The initial velocity of the body is: (g=10ms-2)
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The time used to calculate this velocity is underestimated, leading to a lower velocity.
This option is incorrect. The calculation mistakenly combines vertical and horizontal components inaccurately, resulting in a lower velocity.
C is the correct answer . Find out time first using s=1/2 at^2 (u is 0)t=1suse horizontal motion to find the velocityv=d/tv=10m/s
This option is incorrect. The velocity calculation exceeds realistic values for the given horizontal distance and time, indicating an overestimation.

questions.explanation: The body is projected horizontally, meaning the initial vertical velocity is zero. Use the vertical motion equation s = 1/2 gt2 to find the time of descent, where s = 5m and g = 10ms-2. Solving gives t = 1s.Then, apply the horizontal motion equation v = d/t to determine the initial velocity, with d = 10m and t = 1s. Thus, v = 10ms-1, confirming Option C as correct.Options A and B underestimate the velocity due to incorrect time intervals, while Option D overestimates the velocity, given the constraints of distance and time.

questions.text: A racing car travelling with constant acceleration increases its speed from 10 m/s to 50 m/s over a distance of 60 m. How long does this take?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. By calculating the acceleration first and then using it to find the time taken, 2 seconds is obtained.
This is incorrect. This result comes from a miscalculation. Review the steps for finding both acceleration and time.
This is incorrect. The equations of motion do not support this time given the provided initial and final velocities and distance.
This is incorrect. This time is too long for the given parameters. Re-evaluate the equations used for acceleration and time.

questions.explanation: The scenario describes a uniformly accelerated motion, starting with an initial velocity (u) of 10 m/s and reaching a final velocity (v) of 50 m/s over a distance (s) of 60 m. To find the time taken, we first determine the acceleration using the formula:v2 = u2 + 2as.Rearranging for a, we have: a = (v2 - u2) / (2s) = (502 - 102) / (2 x 60) = 20 m/s2.Next, we use the equation v = u + at to find the time t: t = (v - u) / a = (50 - 10) / 20 = 2 seconds.Therefore, the correct time taken is 2 seconds. Options B, C, and D result from incorrect application of the equations, leading to inaccurate calculations.

questions.text: The distance covered by a body with uniform acceleration in time "t" starting from rest is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the distance covered by the body is given by the formula at^2/2. In this equation, "a" represents the uniform acceleration, and "t" is the time. The formula at^2/2 is known as the equation of motion for uniformly accelerated linear motion when the initial velocity is zero. It describes the distance covered under constant acceleration starting from rest.

This option states that the distance covered by the body is given by the formula vt. In this equation, "v" represents the velocity of the body at time "t." The formula vt is used when the body has a constant velocity throughout its motion. However, in the given question, it is specified that the body has uniform acceleration, not constant velocity. Therefore, this option does not apply to the given scenario.

This option suggests that the distance covered by the body is given by the formula a2t/2. It seems that there might be a typographical error in this option. It is possible that the intended formula was at^2/2, which is the same as Option A. Without additional information or clarification, it is difficult to determine the exact intent of this option.

This option states that both Option A (at^2/2) and Option B (vt) are correct and represent the distance covered by the body. However, as explained earlier, Option B is not applicable to a scenario with uniform acceleration starting from rest.

questions.explanation: S = vit + 1/2at2 = 0 + 1/2at2 = 1/2at2Option A (at^2/2) is the most appropriate answer, as it represents the distance covered by a body with uniform acceleration starting from rest.

questions.text: A cricket ball is hit so that it travels straight up in air and it acquires 3 s to reach the maximum height. Its initial velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is incorrect. It assumes a different acceleration or time frame not aligned with the given conditions of the problem.
This is the correct initial velocity. Using the formula vi = g × t, where g is 9.8 m/s2 and t is 3 s, the initial velocity is calculated as 29.4 m/s.
This value underestimates the initial velocity required to reach the maximum height in the given time frame.
This value is too low to be consistent with the given acceleration due to gravity and time to maximum height.

questions.explanation: To determine the initial velocity needed for the cricket ball to reach its maximum height in 3 seconds, we use the kinematic equation vi = g × t, where g (acceleration due to gravity) is approximately 9.8 m/s2. Substituting the given time t = 3 s, we find the initial velocity vi to be 29.4 m/s. Option B is correct.Options A, C, and D are incorrect because they assume different scenarios or calculations that don't match the conditions described in the question. These values do not satisfy the relationship between time, gravity, and initial velocity needed to reach the maximum height in 3 seconds.

questions.text: A car moving with an initial velocity of 25 mis north has a constant acceleration of 3 m/s2 south. After 6 s its velocity will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The car's velocity decreases as it accelerates in the opposite direction, resulting in a final velocity of 7 m/s north.
This option incorrectly assumes acceleration in the same direction as the initial velocity, which would increase the speed.
This option incorrectly assumes that the acceleration has reversed the car's direction within the given time.
This option does not correctly apply the formula for final velocity, underestimating the effect of the opposing acceleration.

questions.explanation: The final velocity of the car can be calculated using the equation: vf = vi + a·t, where vi is the initial velocity, a is the acceleration, and t is the time.Given: vi = 25 m/s north, a = -3 m/s2 (since it's south), and t = 6 s.Substitute these values into the formula: vf = 25 + (-3) * 6 = 25 - 18 = 7 m/s north.Therefore, the correct answer is 7 m/s north.Option B assumes an increase in velocity, which is incorrect because the acceleration is in the opposite direction. Option C suggests a reversal in direction, which hasn't occurred in the given time frame. Option D underestimates the effect of the opposing acceleration.

questions.text: For a freely falling body, the vertical velocity at the fifth second is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This calculation assumes an incorrect time or acceleration value. The velocity after 5 seconds of free fall with an acceleration of 9.8 ms-2 should be 49 ms-1. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The acceleration for free fall is 9.8 ms-2 a = (Vf - Vi)/t (The object was at rest before it started falling. So, initial velocity Vi is 0) at = Vf - Vi (9.8 x 5) = Vf - 0 Vf = 49 ms-1
This value is calculated for a shorter duration of free fall. At 5 seconds, the correct velocity should be 49 ms-1. Hence, this option is incorrect.
This value is too high for a 5-second free fall. The correct velocity should be 49 ms-1. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The acceleration for free fall is 9.8 ms-2 a = (Vf - Vi)/t (The object was at rest before it started falling. So, initial velocity Vi is 0) at = Vf - Vi (9.8 x 5) = Vf - 0 Vf = 49 ms-1

questions.text: Velocity of an object dropped from a building at any instant "t" is given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This formula represents the distance fallen, not the velocity of the object. The velocity of an object in free fall is not determined by this equation. Hence, this option is incorrect.

This equation calculates the distance traveled for an object with initial velocity vi. Since the object is dropped, the initial velocity is zero, and this equation does not directly provide the velocity at any instant. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
This expression does not relate to the velocity of an object in free fall. It does not incorporate gravitational acceleration (g), and thus it is incorrect for determining velocity.

This is the correct expression for velocity at any instant 't' for an object dropped from rest. The initial velocity is zero, and the acceleration is due to gravity, making the velocity at time 't' equal to gt.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is gt. When an object is dropped from rest, its initial velocity (vi) is zero. The acceleration acting on the object is due to gravity (g). Thus, the velocity at any time 't' is given by the equation Vf = Vi + at, where 'a' is replaced by 'g'. Since Vi is zero when the object is dropped, the equation simplifies to Vf = gt.Option A (1/2 gt2) calculates the distance fallen, not the velocity. Option B (vit + 1/2 gt2) is a formula for distance with initial velocity, which does not directly give velocity. Option C (t2) lacks any consideration of gravitational acceleration and hence does not represent velocity.

questions.text: Which graph shows the motion of a heavy, steel ball falling from a height of 2 m?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It shows that acceleration is uniform which is true in case of free fall. In case of free fall, the acceleration is due to gravity which has a constant value i.e. g=9.8m/s².
It shows that acceleration is variable.
The distance is decreasing with increase in time.
It shows that the velocity is variable.

questions.explanation: It shows that acceleration is uniform which is true in case of free fall. Explanation is given below.

questions.text: A rocket of mass 120 kg is fired in the gravity free space. It ejects gases with velocity 600 m/s at the rate of 1 kg/s. What will be the initial acceleration of the rocket?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The acceleration is not calculated correctly using the given rate of gas ejection and velocity.
This is the correct answer. The acceleration is calculated using the formula for thrust in a gravity-free space.
This option is incorrect. The acceleration value is overestimated based on the given data.
This option is incorrect. The calculation does not match the thrust produced by the specified rate of gas ejection and velocity.

questions.explanation: To calculate the initial acceleration of the rocket, use the rocket thrust formula in the absence of gravity: F = ve * dm/dt, where ve is the exhaust velocity (600 m/s) and dm/dt is the rate of mass ejection (1 kg/s). Therefore, the thrust F is 600 N.Using Newton's second law, F = m * a, where m is the mass of the rocket (120 kg), we find the acceleration a by rearranging the equation to a = F/m. Substituting the values, we get a = 600 N / 120 kg = 5 m/s2.Options A, C, and D are incorrect as they do not correctly apply the formula or misinterpret the data provided in the question.

questions.text: A baseball is thrown vertically into the air. The acceleration of the ball at its highest point is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While the velocity of the ball is zero at its highest point, the acceleration is not zero as gravity continues to act on the ball.
Acceleration due to gravity always acts downwards, with a value of approximately g = 9.8 m/s². This is true even when the ball is at its highest point.
Gravity always acts downwards, not upwards, so this option is incorrect.
The acceleration due to gravity is a constant g, not 2g. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: At the highest point of its trajectory, the ball's velocity is zero, but the acceleration due to gravity is still acting on it, pulling it downwards with a constant acceleration of approximately g = 9.8 m/s². Therefore, the correct answer is 'g, down'. The other options are incorrect because: Option A suggests zero acceleration, which is only true for velocity, not for acceleration due to gravity; Option C suggests an upward acceleration, which contradicts the direction of gravitational force; Option D suggests a doubled gravitational acceleration, which is not the case as gravity remains constant.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct for an object released from rest?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The average velocity during the first second of time is 4.9 m/s
options.explanation: This option correctly states that for an object released from rest, the average velocity during the first second is 4.9 m/s. This is calculated by averaging the initial velocity (0 m/s) and the final velocity after one second (9.8 m/s).

options.text: During each second, the object falls 9.8 m
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the distance fallen increases with time due to acceleration. The object falls 4.9 m in the first second, not 9.8 m.

options.text: The acceleration changes by 9.8 m/s2 every second
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The acceleration remains constant at 9.8 m/s2 for an object in free fall near the Earth's surface.

options.text: The object falls 9.8 m during the first second of time
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because, for an object released from rest, the distance fallen during the first second is 4.9 m, not 9.8 m.

questions.explanation: For an object released from rest, the initial velocity (vi) is 0. Using the equation of motion for uniform acceleration, the final velocity (vf) after time t is given by vf = vi + gt, where g is the acceleration due to gravity (9.8 m/s2). After 1 second, vf = 0 + (9.8)(1) = 9.8 m/s. The average velocity (vav) during this time is (vi + vf) / 2 = (0 + 9.8) / 2 = 4.9 m/s.Therefore, Option A is correct. Option B is incorrect because the object does not fall 9.8 m in each second; the distance increases with time. Option C is incorrect as the acceleration is constant, not changing. Option D is incorrect because the object falls only 4.9 m during the first second.

questions.text: An object is thrown vertically upward with a certain initial velocity in a planet where the acceleration due to gravity is 19.6 m/s2. The height to which it raises is _ that which the object would rise if thrown upward with the same initial velocity on the Earth. Neglect friction.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is correct because on a planet with twice the gravitational acceleration as Earth, the same initial velocity results in half the maximum height.
This option is incorrect. If the gravitational acceleration is higher, the object will not reach twice the height; in fact, it will reach a lower height.
This option is incorrect. The square root relationship does not apply here when considering gravitational acceleration and height.
This option is incorrect. The object would not reach four times the height on a planet with stronger gravity.

questions.explanation: To solve this problem, we use the equation for the maximum height reached by an object thrown vertically upward: h = v02 / (2g), where v0 is the initial velocity and g is the acceleration due to gravity. On Earth, assume g = 9.8 m/s2. On the other planet, g = 19.6 m/s2.Thus, the ratio of the heights is:hplanet / hearth = (v02 / (2 * 19.6)) / (v02 / (2 * 9.8)) = 1/2Therefore, the height reached on the other planet is half of that on Earth. The correct answer is Half. The other options are incorrect as they do not correctly reflect the inverse relationship between gravitational acceleration and maximum height.

questions.text: A bullet shot straight up returns to its starting point in 10 s. Its initial speed was:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the acceleration due to gravity, not the initial velocity of the bullet.
This is the correct initial speed, calculated using the time to reach the peak and the acceleration due to gravity.
This is half of the correct initial speed, likely due to misunderstanding the time to peak altitude.
This value is twice the correct speed, possibly due to doubling the calculated result incorrectly.

questions.explanation: To solve this problem, we first note that the total time for the bullet to return to its starting point is 10 seconds. This means it takes 5 seconds to reach the highest point of its trajectory.Using the equation for uniformly accelerated motion, vf = vi + at, and considering that at the highest point the final velocity vf is 0, we can rearrange the equation to find the initial velocity vi: vi = gt.Here, g is the acceleration due to gravity, approximately 9.8 ms-2, and t is 5 seconds (time to reach the highest point). Therefore, vi = 9.8 ms-2 x 5 s = 49 ms-1.The other options are incorrect: 9.8 ms-1 is the value of g, not the initial speed; 24.5 ms-1 is half of the correct answer, indicating confusion with time; and 98 ms-1 is double the correct initial speed, likely due to miscalculation.

questions.text: If a car at rest accelerates uniformly to a speed of 144km/h in 20 s. Then it covers a distance of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The distance covered is much greater due to the uniform acceleration over 20 seconds.
Incorrect. This distance does not result from the correct application of equations of motion.
This is correct. Convert the speed to m/s, calculate acceleration using the first equation of motion, and then use the second equation of motion to find the distance.
Incorrect as this value is a result of miscalculation or incorrect application of the equations of motion.

questions.explanation: The car starts from rest, so the initial velocity (u) is 0 m/s. The final velocity (v) is 144 km/h, which converts to 40 m/s. The time (t) is 20 seconds. Using the first equation of motion, v = u + at, we calculate the acceleration (a) as 2 m/s². Then, using the second equation of motion, s = ut + 0.5at², we find the distance covered (s) is 400 m. Option C is the correct answer. Options A, B, and D are incorrect as they result from incorrect calculations or application of the formulas.

questions.text: A ball is thrown vertically upward with the velocity of 98 m/sec, how high does the ball rise?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The calculation mistake occurs in the application of the third equation of motion.Using the correct formula: 2aS = vf2 - vi2, where vf = 0, vi = 98 m/s, and a = -9.8 m/s2, the correct height is 490 m.
This option is incorrect. It suggests a drastic underestimation of the ball's maximum height.Re-evaluating with the third equation of motion shows that the correct height is 490 m.
This option is also incorrect and significantly underestimates the height.The correct application of the third equation of motion results in a height of 490 m.
This is the correct option. Applying the third equation of motion, 2aS = vf2 - vi2, with vf = 0, vi = 98 m/s, and a = -9.8 m/s2, gives S = 490 m.

This option is incorrect. It might be misleading as it matches the initial velocity, but does not represent the maximum height.The correct height calculated from the third equation of motion is 490 m.

questions.explanation: s=u(t) + (at2)/2s= 98(t) - 4.9t20 = 98 - 9.81(t)t= 10 ss= 980 -490 = 490m This is the maximum height .

questions.text: A body moves from rest with a constant acceleration of 5 m/s². Its instantaneous speed at the end of 10s is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.

questions.explanation: Using the 1st equation of motion: vf = vi + at vi = 0 m/s a = 5 m/s2 t = 10 s vf = ? Putting in values: vf = 0 + (5)(10) vf = 50 m/s

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an example of accelerated motion?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The vertical component of projectile motion is affected by gravity, which causes a continuous change in velocity, indicating accelerated motion.
A swinging pendulum is subject to gravitational forces that alter its speed and direction, making it a classic example of accelerated motion.
Although the speed remains constant, the direction of a body in circular motion continuously changes, resulting in centripetal acceleration.
In the absence of air resistance, the horizontal component of projectile motion maintains a constant velocity, with no change in speed or direction, making it non-accelerated motion.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the horizontal component of projectile motion. This component maintains a constant velocity as there are no external forces like air resistance or gravity acting horizontally in ideal conditions. Thus, it is not accelerated.In contrast, the vertical component of projectile motion is accelerated due to the force of gravity, which changes its velocity over time. A swinging pendulum is constantly accelerating as it changes speed and direction under the influence of gravity. Circular motion, even at a constant speed, involves centripetal acceleration due to its continuous change in direction.

questions.text: A racing car travelling with constant acceleration increases its speed from 10 m/s to 50 m/s over a distance of 60 m. How long does this take?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. By calculating the acceleration first and then using it to find the time taken, 2 seconds is obtained.
This is incorrect. This result comes from a miscalculation. Review the steps for finding both acceleration and time.
This is incorrect. The equations of motion do not support this time given the provided initial and final velocities and distance.
This is incorrect. This time is too long for the given parameters. Re-evaluate the equations used for acceleration and time.

questions.explanation: The scenario describes a uniformly accelerated motion, starting with an initial velocity (u) of 10 m/s and reaching a final velocity (v) of 50 m/s over a distance (s) of 60 m. To find the time taken, we first determine the acceleration using the formula:v2 = u2 + 2as.Rearranging for a, we have: a = (v2 - u2) / (2s) = (502 - 102) / (2 x 60) = 20 m/s2.Next, we use the equation v = u + at to find the time t: t = (v - u) / a = (50 - 10) / 20 = 2 seconds.Therefore, the correct time taken is 2 seconds. Options B, C, and D result from incorrect application of the equations, leading to inaccurate calculations.

questions.text: A cricket ball is hit so that it travels straight up in air and its acquires 3 seconds to each ball to reach the maximum height. Its initial velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Using the equation v = u + at, where the final velocity v at maximum height is 0, does not give this result.
This is incorrect. Calculating using v = u + at with an acceleration of -9.8ms-2 for 3 seconds does not lead to this initial velocity.
v=u+at (v is 0 at max height) -u=at -u=-9.8 x3 u= 29.4m/s
This is incorrect. Solving the equation v = u + at with the given conditions does not yield this initial velocity.

questions.explanation: To determine the initial velocity of the cricket ball, we use the equation of motion: v = u + at. At the maximum height, the final velocity v is 0 m/s. Given that the ball takes 3 seconds to reach this height and the acceleration due to gravity a is -9.8ms-2, we can rearrange the equation to find the initial velocity u:0 = u + (-9.8ms-2)(3s)u = 9.8ms-2 * 3s = 29.4ms-1Thus, the initial velocity is 29.4ms-1. Other options are incorrect as they do not satisfy the equation with the given values.

questions.text: Suppose you drop an object from the roof of your house. It takes 2 sec. to reach the ground. What is the height of your house?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This height is calculated using a different time or acceleration value, not applicable here.
Correct! Using the equation s = 1/2 * g * t² with g = 9.8 m/s² and t = 2 s, you get 20 meters.
This height is too low for the time and acceleration given.
This height is underestimating the distance fallen in 2 seconds.
The information given is sufficient to solve for height using the equation of motion for free fall.

questions.explanation: To find the height, use the equation of motion for a freely falling object: s = 1/2 * g * t², where s is the height, g is the acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s²), and t is the time in seconds. Plugging in the values, s = 1/2 * 9.8 * (2)² = 19.6 meters, which rounds to approximately 20 meters. Option B is correct. The other options do not yield the correct height based on the given time and gravitational constant.

questions.text: A bullet with a velocity of 100 m/s can just penetrate 2 planks of equal thickness. What is the number of planks penetrated if the velocity of the bullet is doubled?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the velocity is doubled, the kinetic energy increases four times, allowing the bullet to penetrate more than four planks. Hence, this option underestimates the penetration capability.
While this option suggests increased penetration, it doesn't account for the full impact of the kinetic energy increase when velocity is doubled.
This is the correct answer. Doubling the velocity quadruples the kinetic energy, allowing the bullet to penetrate four times the original number of planks, i.e., 8 planks.
This overestimates the penetration capacity of the bullet when the velocity is doubled, as the energy increase is only fourfold, not fivefold.

questions.explanation: When the bullet's velocity is doubled, its kinetic energy becomes four times greater since kinetic energy is proportional to the square of velocity. Initially, the bullet penetrates 2 planks with a velocity of 100 m/s. If the velocity is doubled to 200 m/s, the energy allows for four times the penetration, resulting in the bullet penetrating 8 planks.Other options underestimate or overestimate the penetration capacity based on incorrect assumptions about the relationship between velocity and kinetic energy.

questions.text: A cyclist is travelling at 15 ms-1, she applies brakes so that she doesn't collide with the wall in front of her at a distance of 18 m. Calculate the magnitude of deceleration.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Initial velocity, J = 15m / sFinal velocity, v = 0m / sDistance, s = 18m, Acceleration, a=?Using relation, v2 - u2 = 2as 02 - (15)2= 2a×18- 225 = 36aa = -225/ 36 = - 6.25 m /s2So, deceleration is 6.25m/s² which is equal to 6.3m/s².

This is incorrect as per explanation.Initial velocity, J = 15m / sFinal velocity, v = 0m / sDistance, s = 18m, Acceleration, a=?Using relation, v2 - u2 = 2as 02 - (15)2= 2a×18- 225 = 36aa = -225/ 36 = - 6.25 m /s2So, deceleration is 6.25m/s² which is equal to 6.3m/s².

This is incorrect as per explanation.Initial velocity, J = 15m / sFinal velocity, v = 0m / sDistance, s = 18m, Acceleration, a=?Using relation, v2 - u2 = 2as 02 - (15)2= 2a×18- 225 = 36aa = -225/ 36 = - 6.25 m /s2So, deceleration is 6.25m/s² which is equal to 6.3m/s².

This is incorrect as per explanation.Initial velocity, J = 15m / sFinal velocity, v = 0m / sDistance, s = 18m, Acceleration, a=?Using relation, v2 - u2 = 2as 02 - (15)2= 2a×18- 225 = 36aa = -225/ 36 = - 6.25 m /s2So, deceleration is 6.25m/s² which is equal to 6.3m/s².

questions.explanation: Initial velocity, u=15m/sFinal velocity, v=0m/sDistance, s=18mAcceleration, a=?Using relation, v2 −u2 =2as02 −(15)2 =2a×18−225=36aa= 36 −225 ​ =−6.25m/s2So, deceleration is 6.25m/s2 which is equal 6.3m/s2

questions.text: A ball is thrown vertically upward with the velocity of 98 m/sec, how high does the ball rise?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The calculation mistake occurs in the application of the third equation of motion.Using the correct formula: 2aS = vf2 - vi2, where vf = 0, vi = 98 m/s, and a = -9.8 m/s2, the correct height is 490 m.
This option is incorrect. It suggests a drastic underestimation of the ball's maximum height.Re-evaluating with the third equation of motion shows that the correct height is 490 m.
This option is also incorrect and significantly underestimates the height.The correct application of the third equation of motion results in a height of 490 m.
This is the correct option. Applying the third equation of motion, 2aS = vf2 - vi2, with vf = 0, vi = 98 m/s, and a = -9.8 m/s2, gives S = 490 m.

This option is incorrect. It might be misleading as it matches the initial velocity, but does not represent the maximum height.The correct height calculated from the third equation of motion is 490 m.

questions.explanation: s=u(t) + (at2)/2s= 98(t) - 4.9t20 = 98 - 9.81(t)t= 10 ss= 980 -490 = 490m This is the maximum height .

questions.text: An object with an initial velocity of 40m/s moves along a straight axis under constant acceleration. Three seconds later,its velocity is 14m/s. How far did it travel during this time?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct. By using the kinematic equation, the distance can be calculated as follows: Distance (s) = initial velocity (u) * time (t) + 0.5 * acceleration (a) * t2. Here, you first need to find acceleration using the change in velocity and time.
This is incorrect. It does not align with the expected distance calculations based on the initial and final velocities over the given time.
This is incorrect. Although it may seem plausible, it does not fit the calculations derived from the motion equations.
This answer is incorrect. The result does not match what is expected when applying the kinematic equations correctly.
This is incorrect as it represents a speed rather than a distance. The calculations for distance do not yield this result.

questions.explanation: To solve for the distance traveled, we first need to determine the acceleration. Using the formula for acceleration (a = (final velocity - initial velocity) / time), we have:a = (14 m/s - 40 m/s) / 3 s = -8.67 m/s².Next, we can use the distance formula:s = ut + 0.5at².Substituting the values:s = (40 m/s * 3 s) + 0.5 * (-8.67 m/s²) * (3 s)² = 120 m - 39 m = 81 m.Therefore, the correct answer is 81 m.The other options do not satisfy the kinematic equations for this scenario.

questions.text: An object is dropped from the top of the building. If it takes 10 seconds to hit the ground, what is the height of the building?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option underestimates the height due to incorrect calculations.
The correct answer as calculated using the equation h = vixt + ½gt² with vi = 0 and g = 9.8 m/s².
This option overestimates the height and results from a calculation error.
This option is much higher than the actual height due to a miscalculation.
Far too high, indicating a significant error in applying the formula.

questions.explanation: The object is dropped, meaning its initial velocity vi is zero. Using the equation for uniformly accelerated motion h = vixt + ½gt², and substituting t = 10s and g = 9.8 m/s², we calculate the height: h = 0*10 + ½*(9.8)*(10)² = 490 m. Thus, option B is correct.Options A, C, D, and E result from miscalculations or incorrect application of the formula, leading to values that are either too low or too high.

questions.text: A cyclist is moving towards the right with an acceleration of 4m/sec2. At t=0, he has traveled 5m moving towards the right at 15 m/sec. What will be his position at t = 2 seconds?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The calculation of the position does not account for the correct initial position and velocity.
This option is incorrect. It represents only the displacement from the initial velocity without adding the initial position.
This option is incorrect. The position calculation does not accurately incorporate the acceleration effect over the given time.
This is the correct option. The cyclist's position at t=2 seconds is calculated by considering initial position, velocity, and acceleration.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 43 m. To find the position of the cyclist at t=2 seconds, we use the second equation of motion: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at². Here, x₀ is the initial position (5 m), v₀ is the initial velocity (15 m/s), a is the acceleration (4 m/s²), and t is the time (2 s).Substituting the values, we get:x = 5 + 15(2) + ½(4)(2²) = 5 + 30 + 8 = 43 m.Option A (36) and Option C (41) do not account for the correct combination of initial position, velocity, and acceleration. Option B (38) is the displacement from initial velocity only, not the final position.

questions.text: An object is falling down with a speed of 20 m/s. After 3 secondsits velocity will be _ m/s (g = 10 m/s2).
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. It suggests the object has decelerated, which contradicts the scenario where gravity is accelerating the object.
This is correct. The object's velocity increases by 30 m/s due to gravity over 3 seconds.
This option is incorrect. It overestimates the increase in velocity by adding an extra 5 m/s.
This option is incorrect. It vastly overestimates the increase in velocity, confusing the scenario with one involving much higher acceleration or initial velocity.

questions.explanation: To find the final velocity of the object after 3 seconds of free fall, we use the kinematic equation: v = u + at. The initial velocity (u) is 20 m/s, the acceleration due to gravity (a) is 10 m/s2, and the time (t) is 3 seconds. Substituting these values into the equation, we get v = 20 + 10(3) = 20 + 30 = 50 m/s. Thus, the correct answer is 50 m/s. Option A (05) incorrectly assumes deceleration, Option C (55) miscalculates the velocity increase, and Option D (95) is an extreme overestimate.

questions.text: How large a force is required to accelerate a body of weight 5 N with 4 ms-2 (g 10 ms-2):
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The calculation shows that the force required is 2 N, not 10 N. The mass is calculated as 0.5 kg, and using F = ma, the force is (0.5 kg)(4 ms-2) = 2 N.
This is the correct answer. The mass of the object is calculated from its weight using the equation W = mg, giving m = 5 N / 10 ms-2 = 0.5 kg. Then using F = ma, the force is (0.5 kg)(4 ms-2) = 2 N.
This option is incorrect. The calculation shows that the force required is 2 N. The mass is 0.5 kg, and using F = ma, the force is (0.5 kg)(4 ms-2) = 2 N.
This option is incorrect. The calculation shows that the force required is 2 N. The mass is 0.5 kg, and using F = ma, the force is (0.5 kg)(4 ms-2) = 2 N.

questions.explanation: To solve this problem, we start by determining the mass of the object using its weight. The weight (W) is given as 5 N, and gravitational acceleration (g) is 10 ms-2. Using the formula W = mg, we find the mass (m) as follows: m = W/g = 5 N / 10 ms-2 = 0.5 kg.Next, apply Newton's second law of motion, F = ma, where F is the force, m is the mass (0.5 kg), and a is the acceleration (4 ms-2). Substituting the values, we get F = (0.5 kg)(4 ms-2) = 2 N.Therefore, the correct answer is 2 N. The other options are incorrect because they do not align with the calculation: 10 N overestimates the required force, while 5 N and 1 N underestimate it.

questions.text: Two bodies of unequal mass, placed at rest on a frictionless surface, are acted on by equal horizontal forces for equal times. Just after these forces are removed, the body of greater mass will have:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to Newton's Second Law, F = ma, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass when force is constant. The body with the greater mass will have a smaller acceleration.
This is incorrect because acceleration is inversely proportional to mass. With the same force, the body with the greater mass will have a smaller acceleration, not greater.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity. Given that the force is the same, the body with greater mass will have greater momentum if the durations of force application are equal.
This is incorrect in the context of acceleration. While greater mass means greater momentum if velocities are equal, the question focuses on acceleration, not momentum.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, F = ma or a = F/m. When two bodies of unequal mass are subjected to equal forces, the acceleration is inversely proportional to the mass. Therefore, the body with the greater mass will have a smaller acceleration. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as they either misinterpret the relationship between mass and acceleration or confuse acceleration with momentum.

questions.text: Newton's laws do not hold good for particles:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Newton's first law of motion addresses objects that are at rest or moving with constant velocity. Newton's laws apply perfectly well to objects at rest. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Move with velocity comparable to the speed of light
options.explanation: Newton's laws become inadequate for objects moving at speeds comparable to the speed of light. In such cases, relativistic mechanics developed by Albert Einstein are necessary. Therefore, this is the correct option.
Newton's laws are applicable to objects moving at any speed below the speed of light, including slow-moving objects. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Newton's laws apply to objects moving at high speeds, as long as those speeds are not comparable to the speed of light. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Newton's laws of motion are foundational for understanding the behavior of objects at everyday speeds. However, they do not apply to particles moving at speeds approaching the speed of light, where relativistic effects become significant. In such cases, Einstein's theory of relativity provides the necessary framework. Options A, C, and D describe situations where Newton's laws are still applicable, making them incorrect.

questions.text: A swimmer of mass 60 kg dives into a deep pool from a high diving platform. She enters the water at high speed and then decelerates at a rate of 16 m/s2 as she travels vertically downwards in the water. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. What is the swimmer's weight and the size of the resultant force acting on her as she decelerates?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per the formula.
Check the unit of weight given is not correct which should be newtons
Weight= mg =(60)(10) = 600NResultant force= ma (60)(16)=916N
Incorrect as per the formula.

questions.explanation: Weight of the swimmer = mg = (60) (10) = 600 NResultant force = ma (60) (16) = 960 N

questions.text: When we jump out a boat standing in water, it moves
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because, according to Newton's third law of motion, the force exerted by the person jumping forward results in an equal and opposite reaction force on the boat, causing it to move backward.
This option is incorrect. The boat cannot move forward because the person jumping forward applies a backward force on the boat, resulting in it moving in the opposite direction.
This option is incorrect. Sideways movement would require a lateral force, which is not present in the action of jumping directly out of the boat.
This option is incorrect. The scenario clearly describes an action and reaction pair according to Newton's third law, which dictates that the boat will move backward.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's third law of motion, every action has an equal and opposite reaction. When a person jumps out of a boat in water, they exert a force on the boat in the opposite direction of their jump. This causes the boat to move backward. The backward movement is the reaction to the force exerted by the jumper. The other options are incorrect because they do not adhere to the principles of action and reaction forces as described by Newton's third law.

questions.text: A wooden block of mass 0.60 kg is on a rough horizontal surface. A force of 12 N is applied to the block and it accelerates at 4.0 ms-2. What is the magnitude of the frictional force acting on the block?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to data, above option is incorrect.
According to Newton's 2nd law of motion:Net force = maForward force - frictional force = ma12 - frictional force = 0.60 x 4frictional force = 12 - 2.4frictional force = 9.6 N
According to data, above option is incorrect.
According to data, above option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's 2nd law of motion:Net force = maForward force - frictional force = ma12 - frictional force = 0.60 x 4frictional force = 12 - 2.4frictional force = 9.6 N

questions.text: Action and reaction forces act on:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect: Newton's Third Law of Motion specifies that action and reaction forces act on different objects, not the same object.
Correct: According to Newton's Third Law, action and reaction forces are equal and opposite but act on different objects, ensuring they do not cancel each other out.
Incorrect: While forces can interact with the environment, Newton's Third Law specifically addresses the relationship between two interacting objects, not the environment.
Incorrect: Newton's Third Law clearly states that action and reaction forces act on different objects, making this option inaccurate.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Different objects. Newton's Third Law of Motion states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This implies that action and reaction forces act on two separate objects. This is why the forces do not cancel each other out, allowing motion to occur. The other options are incorrect because they either propose that forces act on the same object, involve the environment incorrectly, or suggest uncertainty where there is none due to the clear principles outlined by Newton's Third Law.

questions.text: While moving a boat forward we have to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Pushing water forward (A) is incorrect because action and reaction are equal and opposite. Pushing water forward would result in an equal force pushing the boat backward due to Newton's third law.

According to Newton's third law of motion, every action has an equal and opposite reaction. So if a boatman wants to move the boat ahead in water, he has to push the water backwards. By pushing the water, the boatman applies a force on the water in a backward direction. In turn, water applies the same amount of force in forward direction, moving the boat ahead.
Keeping the water steady (C) is incorrect as there needs to be a relative motion between the boat and the water to generate a forward thrust. Still water wouldn't propel the boat.
Keeping the water undisturbed (D) is incorrect since moving the boat requires a disturbance in the water, creating a reaction that propels the boat forward, in accordance with Newton's third law.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's third law of motion, every action has an equal and opposite reaction. So if a boatman wants to move the boat ahead in water, he has to push the water backwards. By pushing the water, the boatman applies a force on the water in a backward direction. In turn, water applies the same amount of force in forward direction, moving the boat ahead.

questions.text: A rope of length 5 m is kept on a frictionless surface and a force of 5 N is applied to one of its ends. What is the tension in the rope at 1 m from this end?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Force applied on the rope = 5 NLength of the rope = 5 mAssume x = mass of the rope.If mass of full 5 meters = x, then mass of 1 meter = x / 5F = ma (where F = force, m = mass, a = acceleration)Acceleration of the rope = a = F / m = 5 / xAt 1 meter from the end where the force is applied, tension then acts on the remaining 4 meters​Mass of the 4 meters of rope = 4x / 5​Tension in 4 meters of rope = ma = (4x/5) (5/x) = 4 N

Force applied on the rope = 5 NLength of the rope = 5 mAssume x = mass of the rope.If mass of full 5 meters = x, then mass of 1 meter = x / 5F = ma (where F = force, m = mass, a = acceleration)Acceleration of the rope = a = F / m = 5 / xAt 1 meter from the end where the force is applied, tension then acts on the remaining 4 metersMass of the 4 meters of rope = 4x / 5Tension in 4 meters of rope = ma = (4x/5) (5/x) = 4 N

Force applied on the rope = 5 NLength of the rope = 5 mAssume x = mass of the rope.If mass of full 5 meters = x, then mass of 1 meter = x / 5F = ma (where F = force, m = mass, a = acceleration)​Acceleration of the rope = a = F / m = 5 / xAt 1 meter from the end where the force is applied, tension then acts on the remaining 4 metersMass of the 4 meters of rope = 4x / 5​Tension in 4 meters of rope = ma = (4x/5) (5/x) = 4 N

questions.explanation: Force applied on the rope = 5 NLength of the rope = 5 mAssume x = mass of the rope.If mass of full 5 meters = x, then mass of 1 meter = x / 5F = ma (where F = force, m = mass, a = acceleration)​Acceleration of the rope = a = F / m = 5 / xAt 1 meter from the end where the force is applied, tension then acts on the remaining 4 meters​Mass of the 4 meters of rope = 4x / 5​Tension in 4 meters of rope = ma = (4x/5) (5/x) = 4 N

questions.text: Newton's third law is derived from:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because Newton's first law (law of inertia) states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. It does not directly explain action-reaction forces, which are the core of Newton's third law.
This is incorrect because Newton’s third law is primarily derived from the second law, not the first.
This is incorrect because Newton’s third law can be mathematically derived from Newton’s second law.

This is correct. Newton’s second law states that force is equal to the rate of change of momentum (F=dp/dt). When two objects interact, the force one object exerts on the other changes its momentum. By conservation of momentum, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force, leading to Newton’s third law (for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction).

questions.explanation: Newton's second law tells us that Fnet = 0 and Newton's third law states that to every action, there is always an equal and opposite reaction. Mathematically, FAB = -FBA. When both forces, acting opposite, will be equal then the net force will be zero as implied by the second law.The diagram below simplifies Newton's third law of motion;

questions.text: A rider on a horse back falls when the horse starts running all of a sudden because:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The rider is pushed backward by the air resistance
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Air resistance would not be significant enough to cause the rider to fall backward in this scenario. The primary factor is inertia, not air resistance.

options.text: The rider becomes suddenly afraid due to the horse's movement
options.explanation: This is incorrect. While fear might be a factor psychologically, the physical reason the rider falls is due to inertia, not emotion.

options.text: Inertia of rest keeps the rider's upper body stationary while the lower body moves with the horse
options.explanation: This is correct. When the horse suddenly starts to run, the upper part of the rider's body tends to remain at rest due to inertia, causing the rider to lose balance and fall.
This is incorrect. The correct explanation is related to the inertia of rest affecting the rider's body.

questions.explanation: When the horse starts running suddenly, the rider's body is subject to the law of inertia, which states that an object at rest will remain at rest unless acted upon by an external force. The rider's upper body remains at rest due to inertia while the lower body moves forward with the horse. This difference in motion causes the rider to lose balance and fall. This phenomenon is a direct application of Newton's first law of motion.The incorrect options either misattribute the cause to air resistance or emotion, which are not the primary factors in this physical scenario.

questions.text: A balloon is acted upon by three force, weight, upthrust and sideway force due to the wind, as shown in the diagramWhat is the vertical component of the resultant force on the balloon?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the value of the sideways (horizontal) force acting due to wind. However, the question specifically asks for the vertical component of the resultant force. So this option is incorrect because it refers to the wrong axis (horizontal instead of vertical).

Why it’s correct:The vertical forces acting are:Upthrust (upward) = 9000 NWeight (downward) = 8000 NThe net vertical force = 9000 N - 8000 N = 1000 N (upward).So, this is the correct vertical component of the resultant force.

This number might be a confusion from adding all forces (9000 + 8000 + 500) without considering direction or components. But that’s not how resultant forces are calculated — especially when vectors are in different directions. So this is a miscalculated or misinterpreted value.

This seems to come from adding all three forces blindly:9000 (upward) + 8000 (downward) + 500 (sideways) = 17,500Or, possibly, just 9000 (up) + 500 (sideways) + 1000 (net) = 10,500Either way, it is not the vertical component alone and mixes up horizontal and vertical vectors, which is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To find the vertical component of the resultant force, we only consider the upward force (upthrust = 9000 N) and the downward force (weight = 8000 N). Subtracting these gives:9000 N – 8000 N = 1000 N (upward)The sideways force (500 N) acts in the horizontal direction and does not affect the vertical component. Hence, the correct vertical component of the resultant force is:

questions.text: When force of IN is applied on a body of mass 100g then the acceleration would be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. Using Newton's Second Law, this acceleration would require a different force or mass.
Incorrect. This acceleration value does not match the calculated result using F=ma.
Correct. Using F=ma, with F=1N and mass=0.1kg, the acceleration is 10ms-2.
Incorrect. This value does not align with the calculation based on the given force and mass.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, the formula to calculate acceleration is F = ma, where F is the force, m is the mass, and a is the acceleration. Given the force F is 1 N and the mass m is 100 g (which is 0.1 kg in SI units), the acceleration a can be calculated as follows:a = F/m = 1 N / 0.1 kg = 10 ms-2.Therefore, the correct answer is Option C: 10ms-2.Options A, B, and D are incorrect as they do not match the calculated acceleration based on the given force and mass.

questions.text: Which law of motion defines force?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Newton's first law of motion states that "an object remains at rest, or in uniform motion in a straight line, unless compelled to change by an external net force." This describes inertia, not the definition of force.

Newton's second law of motion states that "the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass." This law provides the definition of force as the product of mass and acceleration (F = ma).
Newton's third law of motion states that "for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction." While it describes interactions between forces, it does not define force itself.
Only Newton's second law of motion directly defines force. The other laws describe different aspects of motion and force interactions.

questions.explanation: Newton's second law of motion provides the definition of force by establishing the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration: F = ma. This law indicates that the force applied to an object is equal to the mass of the object multiplied by the acceleration it experiences. The first law describes inertia, and the third law is about action-reaction pairs, neither of which define force itself.
